ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, fried) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for prawn-paste-marinated fried fish slices in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried prawn paste fish slice consists of approximately 4 to 5 battered and deep-fried fish slices, weighing around 150 g, often served as part of a shared zi char meal or as a main dish with rice.

nutrient_source.title: Fried fish fillet with batter — adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15264) and HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database for prawn paste seasoning contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for prawn-paste-marinated fried fish slices exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for battered deep-fried white fish fillet (NDB 15264, 'Fish, battered or breaded, and fried') with an adjustment for prawn paste (belacan) seasoning contribution to sodium. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are anchored to USDA SR Legacy NDB 15264. Sodium is elevated to reflect prawn paste content, consistent with HPB Singapore guidance on fermented shrimp paste dishes. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of battered and fried fish products — analogous estimate based on 'Fish fingers, fried in canola oil' and 'Battered fish fillet, deep-fried' entries from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn-paste-marinated fried fish slices. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous battered and deep-fried fish products in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 38–52 for similar battered fish items. The batter contributes moderate starch; prawn paste seasoning does not materially alter GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (45 × 14.6 × 1.50) / 100 = 9.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from prawn paste (belacan) marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of fried prawn paste fish slice due to its high sodium content (approximately 620 mg per 100 g, equating to ~930 mg per 150 g serving), which can contribute significantly to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg recommended by HPB Singapore; portion control and reduced-frequency consumption are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from batter coating and deep-frying fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful that the wheat-flour batter contributes a moderate glycemic load (~9.9 g GL per serving) and the deep-frying process adds significant fat; portion size should be limited to one or two slices and paired with low-GI vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and fat from deep-frying and prawn paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of high sodium (~620 mg/100 g) and moderate total fat (~12.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying warrants cautious consumption; choosing steamed or grilled fish alternatives and limiting this dish to occasional intake is recommended to support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Prawn Paste Fish Slice (虾酱炸鱼片, Xia Jiang Zha Yu Pian) is a popular Singaporean Chinese zi char dish consisting of white fish fillets — commonly batang (Spanish mackerel), snapper, or dory — marinated in fermented prawn paste (har cheong or belacan), egg, garlic, sesame oil, and white pepper, then coated in a light batter of plain flour and cornstarch before being deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is inspired by the flavour profile of the well-known Singaporean har cheong gai (prawn paste chicken), adapted here for fish. The exterior is crisp and aromatic with the pungent, umami-rich character of fermented shrimp paste, while the interior remains moist and flaky. It is typically served as part of a shared zi char meal alongside steamed rice and stir-fried vegetables. Nutritionally, each 150 g serving provides approximately 342 kcal, 22 g protein, 19 g fat, and 22 g carbohydrate, with elevated sodium (~930 mg per serving) due to the prawn paste marinade. The dish is a moderate source of omega-3 fatty acids from the fish and provides vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and the battered deep-fried preparation increases caloric density compared to steamed or grilled fish. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in controlled portions.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for prawn-paste-marinated fried fish slices was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy battered deep-fried fish data (NDB 15264) with sodium adjusted upward to reflect prawn paste contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar battered fried fish products. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base food category (battered deep-fried fish) is well-represented in approved sources and the prawn paste adjustment is conservative and documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15264 (Fish, battered or breaded, and fried) with sodium elevated to approximately 620 mg/100 g to reflect fermented prawn paste (belacan) marinade contribution, consistent with HPB Singapore guidance on high-sodium fermented condiments. 2. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for battered fried fish — no plant-derived vitamin C source is present in this preparation. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g/100 g, reflecting trace trans fats from deep-frying oil; this is a plausible trace value consistent with USDA data for deep-fried fish products. 4. GI is an analogous estimate of 45 (low) based on battered and deep-fried fish products in the University of Sydney GI Database (range 38–52 for similar items); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 5. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15264 and are approximate. 6. The halal status is marked false because prawn paste (belacan) in Singapore is commonly made with fermented shrimp and may contain non-halal processing agents depending on the brand; consumers requiring halal certification should verify the specific prawn paste used. 7. Price data (SGD 20.40) is preserved from the GrabFood source record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
